explanation:
(A) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952323/preptracker/questions/q_108128_explanation_1764952322745.png

II. Hoffmann bromamide reaction
III. Carbylamine reaction
IV. Sandmeyer reaction
explanation:

(I) Gabriel phthalimide reaction is a chemical process that converts primary alkyl halides into primary amine in presence of strong base.

question:
Arrange the following in the order of decreasing basicity.
I. $\mathrm{RN}=\mathrm{CHR}$
II. $\mathrm{RC} \equiv \mathrm{N}$
III. $\mathrm{RNH}_{2}$
explanation: The sp-hybrid N -atom is most electron withdrawing due to maximum s -character, more electronegative element has less tendency to donate electrons and therefore is least basic. Therefore,  $\mathrm{RC} \equiv \mathrm{N}$ is least basic.  Out of   $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$  and   $\mathrm{RN}=\mathrm{CHR}, \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$  has  $\mathrm{sp}^{3-}$ - hybrid N - atom and less electronegative in nature and therefore is more basic.  Therefore, correct order of decreasing basicity.  $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{CHR}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}$  i.e.  ${\text{III }} > {\text{ I }} > {\text{ II}}.$
subject: Chemistry

question: The test that distinguishes primary amines from other amines is
explanation:
Primary amines can be distinguished from other amines by carbylamine test. Chemical reaction is as follows: $\underset{\substack{\text { Primary } \\ \text { amine }}}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}}+\underset{\text { Chloroform }}{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Potassium } \\ \text { hydroxide }}}{\mathrm{KOH}} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Alkyl } \\ \text { isocyanide }}}{\mathrm{RNC}}$
Secondary and tertiary amines do not give carbylamine test.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Tests to Distinguish Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Amines

question: The correct order of basic strength of the following amines is
explanation:
I. Piperidine is the most basic. The lone pair on nitrogen is in an $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybrid orbital and there is no resonance and due to $ + {\text{I}}$ effect of alkyl group attached, it becomes more basic than $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridised ammonia.
II. In pyridine lone pair is in a $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised orbital but does not get involved in delocalisation. The $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybrid is smaller than $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$, so there is a stronger attraction to nucleus, so less basic.
III. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is more basic as in ammonia, lone pair is localised and easily available for donation. Basic character is determined by ability to donate lone pair.
IV. The basicity of aniline is lower than basicity of ammonia as in aniline, the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is involved in resonance with benzene ring.
So, correct order of basic strength is
I $>$ III $>$ II $>$ IV
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: The order of basicity among the following nitrogen compounds is
explanation: Aryl amines have amine group directly attached to aromatic ring. These are weaker bases than arylalkyl amines due to involvement of lone pair of nitrogen in resonance with benzene ring which decreases the availability of lone pair of electron on nitrogen. So, (i) is more basic. Then $3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$ amines is the basic strength order. Hence the correct order of basicity of amines is $\mathrm{i}>\mathrm{iii}>\mathrm{ii}>\mathrm{iv}$.

question: The correct order of decreasing basic strength of the given amines is:
explanation:
Amines act as bases due to the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. The more available the lone pair is, the stronger is the base. N -ethylethanamine has two ethyl groups attached to the nitrogen, making it the strongest base among the given options.
Ethanamine has one ethyl group attached to the nitrogen, making it slightly weaker than N ethylethanamine.
N -methylaniline has one methyl group attached to the nitrogen making it weaker than ethanamine. Aniline has only a phenyl group attached to the nitrogen, making it the weakest base due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the phenyl group.
subject: Chemistry

question: Correct order of basic strength of Pyrrole
explanation: Piperidine is most basic because the lone pair is present in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{3}}}$ hybrid orbital of N -atom while in pyridine the lone pair is present in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{2}}}$ hybrid orbital of N - atom. Greater the s character of orbital, more strongly the electrons will be bonded with the atom and less will be the basicity. Pyrrole is least basic among the given compounds due to involvement of its lone pair of electrons towards aromatic sextet formation. Hence, the basicity order is piperidine ${\mathrm{>}}$ pyridine ${\mathrm{>}}$ pyrrole. So, the correct option is (4).

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below
All of the following compounds react with p -toluenesulfonyl chloride. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2} ;\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH} ;\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}}}$.
Their products in the above reaction are soluble in aqueous ${\text{NaOH}} \cdot $
explanation: Hinsberg's reagent or p-toluenesulfonyl chloride reacts with ${\mathrm{1^{\circ}}}$ and ${\mathrm{2^{\circ}}}$ amines. The product formed of ${\mathrm{1^{\circ}}}$ amine with Hinsberg's reagent is soluble in aqueous NaOH due to acidic hydrogen bonded to nitrogen. Thus, both the statements are false. So, the correct option is (4).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subtopic: Tests to Distinguish Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Amines

Evolution of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ with $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$, suggests that $1^{\circ}$ amine $59.07 \mathrm{gm}$ of (I) is present in $100\,\,{\rm{gm}}$ of compound
$127\,\,{\rm{gm}}$ of $\mathrm{I}(1 \mathrm{~mol})$ is present in $\dfrac{100 \times 127}{59.07}=215$

question: Which of the following is the correct increasing order of basicity of amines in gaseous phase?
explanation:
Alkyl amines $\left(1^{\circ}, 2^{\circ}\right.$ and $\left.3^{\circ}\right)$ are stronger bases than ammonia. This can be explained in terms of electron releasing inductive effect of alkyl group. As a result, the electron density on the nitrogen atom increases and thus, they can donate the lone pair of electrons more easily than ammonia.
The electron releasing effect is maximum in tertiary amines and minimum in primary amines (in gas phase).
$3^{\circ}$ amine $>2^{\circ}$ amine $>1^{\circ}$ amine $>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question:
Assertion: Basicity of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (I), $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (II) and $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (III) is in the order I $>$ II $>$ III.

Reason: Electron-donating groups (such as alkyl group) increase the basicity of amines, and electron-withdrawing groups (such as aryl group) decrease the basicity of amines.
explanation:
Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.Presence of electron releasing group at benzene nucleus increases the basic character whereas electron withdrawing group decrease the basic character. Hence, correct order of basicity is
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_2} > {\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3} > {{\rm{C}}_6}{{\rm{H}}_5}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_2}$
${\rm{(I)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(II)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(III)}}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: Which of the following statements are correct?
explanation: Only statement (3) is correct, whereas statement ( 1 , 2 and 4 ) are incorrect. Basic character depends upon the availability of lone pair of electrons for donation. Aliphatic amines are generally more basic than aromatic amines because lone pair takes part in delocalisation in case of aromatic amines. Presence of electron releasing group at benzene nucleus increases the basic character of amine. Amino group strongly activates the ring through delocalisation of lone pair of electrons of $\mathrm{N}$-atom over the aromatic ring. Thus, electron density increases more at $o$ - and $p$ - positions as compared to $m$-position.

options.text: Aniline is a stronger base than ethyl amine
Aniline is a stronger base than p-methoxyaniline
Aniline must be acetylated before nitration with an acid derivative
Aniline is soluble in an ammonium hydroxide solution

question: The strongest base among the following is
explanation: Basic nature of amines is due to the presence of lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom which is available for the bond formation with Lewis acid. Due to the +I-effect, $2^{\circ}$ amine is more basic than $1^{\circ}$ amine and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. In case of aromatic amines, the lone pair on nitrogen atom are involved in resonance, therefore not available for bond formation, so aromatic amines are less basic.Hence, $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$ is the strongest base among the given options.

a hydroxyl group in the molecule

question: The correct order of basic strength of the following are
explanation: Electron releasing group $-\mathrm{OH}$ in compound (I) increases the basic strength while electron withdrawing group ( $-\mathrm{COCH}_{3}$ in compound $\mathrm{I}$ and IV) decreases the basic strength of amines. In compound IV, $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ is directly attached to benzene ring, it results in conjugation of unshared pair of electrons with benzene ring and thus making it less available for protonation. Therefore, correct order is III $>$ II $>$ IV $>$ I.

question:
Read Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option given below.

Assertion: p-fluoroanilinium ion is more acidic than anilinium ion.

Reason: Electron density in the N-H bond of p-fluoroanilinium ion decreases and release of a proton from p-fluoroanilinium ion is much easier than from anilinium ion.
explanation:
Due to – I effect of F – atom it withdraws electrons from ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}^{+} \mathrm{H}_{3}}}$ group. As a result, electron density in the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}}$ bond of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{p}-}}$ fluoroanilinium ion decreases and hence release of a proton from p - fluoroanilinium ion is much more easier than from anilinium ion.
So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but reason is true.

question: The correct order in aqueous medium of basic strength in case of methyl substituted amines is
explanation:
In aqueous medium basic strength is dependent on electron density on nitrogen as well as solvation of cation formed after accepting $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ion. Considering the combined effects of solvation and inductive effect, the order of basic strength of methyl amines in the aqueous state is
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
So, the correct option is (3).

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955102/preptracker/questions/q_29076_explanation_1764955102478.png overall reaction is
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NaBr}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
The Hoffmann bromamide degradation of acetamide involves the formation of methyl isocyanate as an intermediate, which, upon hydrolysis, yields methylamine and sodium carbonate.. Thus, option (2) is correct.

Unlike aniline, cyclohexylamine lacks resonance, rendering its nitrogen lone pair more accessible for protonation, making it a stronger base. Given reason is also a correct statement. So the option (2) is correct.

Reason: In p-chloronitrobenzene, both vectors of dipole moment act in opposite direction.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955081/preptracker/questions/q_29008_explanation_1764955080806.png  In p-chloronitrobenzene, the individual dipole moments due to the chlorine atom and nitro group are in opposite directions and hence partially get cancelled. This reduces the overall dipole moment. Whereas, in p-nitrotoluene both the bond dipoles due to the methyl group and the nitro group are in the same direction and get added. So the option (1) is correct.

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955079/preptracker/questions/q_29000_explanation_1764955078520.png It is a method for preparation of primary amines only. $\mathrm{N}$-methyl acetamide $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHCH}_{3}\right)$ is a secondary amide and cannot give Hoffmann bromamide reaction, as it cannot form the reaction intermediate. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Identify the compounds from the following which form primary amines under suitable reduction conditions:
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NC}$ (II) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(III) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ (IV) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
explanation:
Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction gives primary amines.
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+ & 4 \mathrm{KOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2} \\& +2 \mathrm{KBr}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
Primary amines may also be prepared by the reduction of nitroalkanes, nitrobenzene etc.
${\text{RN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow[\substack{ {\text{ or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{LAH}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{/Pt }} \\ {\text{ or Metal}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{acid }} } ]{{{\text{Ni/250}}^\circ {\text{C}}}}{\text{RN}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
${{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ LAH }}}]{{{\text{Ni}}/250^\circ {\text{C}}}}{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$

question: Which among the following reagents is used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary amines according to solubility in alkaline medium?
explanation:
Benzenesulphonyl chloride $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$, (Hinsberg reagent) is used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary amines.
The reaction of benzenesulphonyl chloride with primary amine yields $\mathrm{N}$-alkylbenzene sulphonamide which is soluble in alkali.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{RNH}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{NHR}$
(Soluble in alkali due to presence of acidic hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom.
With secondary amine, it yields N, N dialkylbenzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{NR}_{2}$
(Insoluble in alkali due to absence hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom.
Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl},+\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N} \rightarrow$ No reaction.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Tests to Distinguish Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Amines

question: Correct order of basic strength is
explanation:
Basic nature depends on the ability to donate lone pair of electrons by the atoms present in the species.
Structure (III) is most basic as in that, $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is not directly bonded with the benzene ring. (aliphatic amine)
Structure (II) is 2nd most basic due to presence of $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ group in benzene ring at para position.
Structure (I) is next basic species which is less basic than (III) and (II) but more basic than structure (I) due to presence of electron releasing $\left(-\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)$ group attached to $-\mathrm{NH}$ group. Structure IV is least basic, because
$-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is directly bonded to the benzene ring, thus lone pair of electrons on $\mathrm{N}$ - atom is involved in resonance. Hence correct order is,
(III) $>$ (II) $>$ (I) $>$ (IV) .
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question:
Assertion: Anilinium chloride is more acidic than ammonium chloride.

Reason: Anilinium ion is resonance stabilised.
explanation:
Anilinium chloride is more acidic than ammonium chloride because it forms salt with water and liberates amine when treated with strong base.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{NaCl}$
Anilinium ion does not show resonance because charge dispersion at ring may involve pentavalent nitrogen structure.
Hence, Assertion is correct and Reason is incorrect.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and  Reason are  incorrect.

question: The amine 'A' when treated with nitrous acid gives yellow oily substance. The amine $\mathrm{A}$ is
explanation:
Secondary amines when treated with nitrous acid give yellow oily substance. So, amine ' $\mathrm{A}$ ' is methylphenyl amine.
Reaction of nitrous acid with aliphatic amines liberate $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas along with alcohol.
$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{HNO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \equiv \mathrm{NCl}^{-}$
$\stackrel{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}$
Reaction of nitrous acid with aromatic amines liberates $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas along with phenol.
$\mathrm{PhNH}_{2}+\mathrm{HNIO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \stackrel{273-276 \mathrm{~K}}{\longrightarrow}$
$\mathrm{Ph}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \equiv \mathrm{NCl}^{-1} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PhOH}+\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$
Reaction of nitrous acid with $2^{\circ}$ amines with
$\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$, they give nitroso amines, which being insoluble in dilute acids separte as yellow oily compound.
$\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}+\mathrm{HONO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{N}$-nitroso dialkyl amine (yellow Oil)
Reaction of nitrous acid with $3^{\circ}$ amines
Aliphatic $3^{\circ}$ amines on reaction with $\mathrm{HONO}$ form soluble nitrite salts, while aromatic $3^{\circ}$ amines form green coloured P-nitroso amines.
${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{\,N + HONO}} \to \mathop {\left[ {{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{3}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{H}}} \right]{\rm{NO}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}}\limits_{\left( {{\rm{Soluble}}\,\,{\rm{nitrite}}} \right)} $
${\rm{Ph - NM}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + HONO}} \to $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949243/preptracker/questions/q_28604_explanation_1764949242932.jpg
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Tests to Distinguish Primary, Secondary and Tertiary Amines

question: Which of the following amines is most basic in nature in aqueous phase?
explanation:
Dimethylamine is most basic in nature in aqueous phase. It is more basic as, it is a secondary amine. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is further away from the nucleus at a higher energy level due to extra repulsion from the electrons in each $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N}$ bond, thus the nitrogen atom is more capable at donating a pair of electrons. So, dimethylamine is a strong base in solution (aqueous phase), as more $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions are formed, when nitrogen atom accepts a proton causing the solution to be more alkaline as the $\mathrm{pH}$ higher.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NHCH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$

question: Alkyl cyanides on reduction by sodium and ethanol give primary amines. This reaction is called as
explanation:
${\text{R - }}\mathop {{\text{C}} \equiv {\text{N + 4}}}\limits_{{\text{Alkyl cyanide}}} \left[ {\text{H}} \right]\xrightarrow{{{\text{Na Ethanol}}}}{\text{R - }}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ - N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Primary amine}}} $
Given, reaction is called Mendius reduction. In this reaction the reduction of cyano group $(-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N})$ takes place by nascent hydrogen in the presence of sodium and alcohol. Here nascent hydrogen breaks $\mathrm{X}$ bond of cyano group.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Named Preparation Methods of Amines

question: Select the compound which on treatment with nitrous acid liberates nitrogen.
explanation:
Primary amines on treatment with nitrous acid liberates nitrogen.
Ethylamine $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}\right)$ is a primary amine.
Hence, liberates nitrogen on treatment with nitrous acid.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{HNO}_{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}}{\longrightarrow}\end{aligned}$
${\text{R - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\text{ + }} \equiv {{\text{N}}^{\text{ - }}}}\limits_{\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}{{\text{Diazonium}}} \\ {{\text{chloride}}} \end{array}} {\text{Cl}}\xrightarrow{{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}}{\text{ROH + }}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + HCl}}$

question: The correct order of increasing basic nature for the bases $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ and $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$ in aqueous solutions is
explanation:
Due to $+\mathrm{I}$ effect of methyl group $2^{0}$ amine are more basic than $1^{0}$ amine.
Hence, order of basicity of methyl amine in aqeuous solution is : $\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2} < \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: The basicity of aniline is less than that of cyclohexylamine. This is due to
explanation: $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ has a $+\mathrm{R}$ effect, it donates electrons to the benzene ring. As a result of resonance, the lone pair of electrons on the $\mathrm{N}$ atom gets delocalized over the benzene ring and thus it is less readily available for protonation. Hence aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexyl amine. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949203/preptracker/questions/q_28521_explanation_1764949202942.jpg
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following statement(s) is/are incorrect in case of Hofmann bromamide degradation?
explanation:
Statement (B) is incorrect in case of Hofmann bromanide degradation. This is used to synthesise primary amine.
In this reaction, amide on reaction with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ give $1^{\circ}$ amine with one $\mathrm{C}$ atom less than that present in the amine.
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{RCONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{KOH} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \underset{1^{\circ} \mathrm{a} \text { mine }}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}+2 \mathrm{KBr}} \\+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{gathered}$

options.text: Reaction is useful for decreasing length of carbon chain by one carbon atom

question: Hoffmann's bromamide reaction is to convert
explanation: Hoffmann's bromamide reaction: Amides when heated with bromine and caustic soda or caustic potash solution, yield primary amine containing one carbon atom less than the amide.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question: Which of the following would undergo Hoffmann reaction to give a primary amine?
explanation:
When a primary amide is heated with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{KOH}$ and $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$, it gives a primary amine with one carbon atom less.
$\begin{aligned}& \underset{\text { amide }}{\mathrm{RCONH}_{2}}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{KOH} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \\& \underset{1^{\circ} \text { amine }}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{KBr}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
This is known as Hoffmann degradation reaction.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question: How many primary amines are possible for molecular formula ${{\rm{C}}_{\rm{3}}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{9}}}{\rm{N}}$ ?

question:
Aniline does not undergo alkylation and acetylation.
Nitrogen of aniline acquires positive charge in the presence of $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$
explanation: Aniline does not undergo Friedel Crafts reaction (alkylation and acetylation) due to salt formation with $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$, (the Lewis acid) which is used as a catalyst. Due to this nitrogen of aniline acquires positive charge and hence, act as a strong deactivating group for further reaction.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
Aliphatic amines are weaker bases than ammonia and aromatic amines are stronger bases than ammonia.
+I-effect of alkyl groups on aliphatic amines increase the electron density on nitrogen atom. Aromatic amines are weaker bases due to electron withdrawing nature of the aryl group.
explanation: Aliphatic amines are stronger base than ammonia due to + I-effect of alkyl groups leading to high electron density on the nitrogen atom. Their $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ values lie in the range of 3 to 4.22 which is lower than $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ value of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, i.e. 4.75. On the other hand, aromatic amines are weaker base than ammonia due to the electron withdrawing nature of the aryl group.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

In gaseous phase the basic nature of aliphatic amine increases with the increase in number of alkyl groups.
explanation:
Order of basicity of amines in gaseous phase follows the order:
Tertiary amine $>$ secondary amine $>$ primary amine $>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. The basic nature of aliphatic amines increases with increase in the number of alkyl groups (in gaseous phase).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: An organic compound $\mathrm{A}$ on reacting with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives $\mathrm{B}$. On heating $\mathrm{B}$, it gives $\mathrm{C}$. $\mathrm{C}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{KOH}$ reacts with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ to give $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$. A is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A} \stackrel{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{B} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{C} \stackrel{\mathrm{KOH} / \mathrm{Br}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$
The reaction of $\mathrm{C}$ with $\mathrm{KOH} / \mathrm{Br}_{2}$ to give amine is called Hoffmann bromamide reaction. this reaction is given by acid amides only in which https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/15/ES-Q-44_(1).jpg group undergoes rearrangement along with the loss of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ molecule. Thus, the
compound $\mathrm{C}$ must be acid amide with three carbon atoms. Hence, the compound $\mathrm{C}$ is https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/15/ES-Q-44_(2).jpg Since, all the options show that $\mathrm{A}$ is an acid and it forms acid amide on reaction with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. Thus, acid must contain three carbon atoms. Hence the compound $\mathrm{A}$ is $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$. The complete series of reactions can be represented as
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{COOH}}}\limits_{\left( {\text{A}} \right)} \xrightarrow{{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\text{ + }} {{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{\left( {\text{B}} \right)} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949112/preptracker/questions/q_28253_explanation_1764949111894.jpg

question: A compound with molecular mass $180 u$ is acylated with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}$ to get a compound with molecular mass $390 u$. The number of amino groups present per molecule of the former compound is
explanation:
During acetylation, one $H$ atom with atomic mass 1 amu of $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is replaced by an acetyl group $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$ [molecular mass $=434 u]$. Thus, $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl} \rightarrow-\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}$
The above equation suggests that the acylation of each $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group increases the mass by $42 u[43-1]$. If the molecular mass of the organic compound is $180 u$, while that of the acylated product is $390 u$, then the increase in the mass due to acylation is given by
$390-180=210 u$
Hence, the number of $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ groups $=\dfrac{210 u}{42 u}=5$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Alkylation, Acylation and Benzoylation Reactions

question: Considering the basic strength of amines in aqueous solution, which one has the smallest $p K_{b}$ value?
explanation:
It is known that, $p K_{b}=-\log K_{b}$
Thus, larger the value of $K_{b}$ smaller is the value of $p K_{b}$, hence, stronger is the base.
The order of basic strength of amines is as follows:
${\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{H > C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ > }}{\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{\,}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{ > }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{\ddot H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
The basic strength of amines depends on the inductive effect as well as steric hindrance of alkyl groups. Hence, ${\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{H}}$ [secondary amine] possesses the smallest value of ${\rm{p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

explanation: Aromatic amines do react with acid chlorides to form amides, and the reaction mechanism involves the amine acting as a nucleophile due to its lone pair of electrons, attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the acid chloride. Correct option is (1)

question:
Arrange the following in the order of decreasing basicity.
I. $\mathrm{RN}=\mathrm{CHR}$
II. $\mathrm{RC} \equiv \mathrm{N}$
III. $\mathrm{RNH}_{2}$
explanation: The sp-hybrid N -atom is most electron withdrawing due to maximum s -character, more electronegative element has less tendency to donate electrons and therefore is least basic. Therefore,  $\mathrm{RC} \equiv \mathrm{N}$ is least basic.  Out of   $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$  and   $\mathrm{RN}=\mathrm{CHR}, \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$  has  $\mathrm{sp}^{3-}$ - hybrid N - atom and less electronegative in nature and therefore is more basic.  Therefore, correct order of decreasing basicity.  $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{CHR}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}$  i.e.  ${\text{III }} > {\text{ I }} > {\text{ II}}.$

question: The test that distinguishes primary amines from other amines is
explanation:
Primary amines can be distinguished from other amines by carbylamine test. Chemical reaction is as follows: $\underset{\substack{\text { Primary } \\ \text { amine }}}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}}+\underset{\text { Chloroform }}{\mathrm{CHCl}_{3}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Potassium } \\ \text { hydroxide }}}{\mathrm{KOH}} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Alkyl } \\ \text { isocyanide }}}{\mathrm{RNC}}$
Secondary and tertiary amines do not give carbylamine test.

question: The correct order of basic strength of the following amines is
explanation:
I. Piperidine is the most basic. The lone pair on nitrogen is in an $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybrid orbital and there is no resonance and due to $ + {\text{I}}$ effect of alkyl group attached, it becomes more basic than $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridised ammonia.
II. In pyridine lone pair is in a $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised orbital but does not get involved in delocalisation. The $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybrid is smaller than $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$, so there is a stronger attraction to nucleus, so less basic.
III. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is more basic as in ammonia, lone pair is localised and easily available for donation. Basic character is determined by ability to donate lone pair.
IV. The basicity of aniline is lower than basicity of ammonia as in aniline, the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen is involved in resonance with benzene ring.
So, correct order of basic strength is
I $>$ III $>$ II $>$ IV
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: The order of basicity among the following nitrogen compounds is
explanation: Aryl amines have amine group directly attached to aromatic ring. So, (i) is more basic. Then $3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$ amines is the basic strength order. Hence the correct order of basicity of amines is $\mathrm{i}>\mathrm{iii}>\mathrm{ii}>\mathrm{iv}$.

question: The correct order of decreasing basic strength of the given amines is:
explanation:
Amines act as bases due to the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. The more available the lone pair is, the stronger is the base. N -ethylethanamine has two ethyl groups attached to the nitrogen, making it the strongest base among the given options.
Ethanamine has one ethyl group attached to the nitrogen, making it slightly weaker than N ethylethanamine.
N -methylaniline has one methyl group attached to the nitrogen making it weaker than ethanamine. Aniline has only a phenyl group attached to the nitrogen, making it the weakest base due to the electron-withdrawing effect of the phenyl group.

question: Correct order of basic strength of Pyrrole
explanation: Piperidine is most basic because the lone pair is present in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{3}}}$ hybrid orbital of N -atom while in pyridine the lone pair is present in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{sp}^{2}}}$ hybrid orbital of N - atom. Greater the s character of orbital, more strongly the electrons will be bonded with the atom and less will be the basicity. Pyrrole is least basic among the given compounds due to involvement of its lone pair of electrons towards aromatic sextet formation. question: Which of the following is the correct increasing order of basicity of amines in gaseous phase?
explanation:
Alkyl amines $\left(1^{\circ}, 2^{\circ}\right.$ and $\left.3^{\circ}\right)$ are stronger bases than ammonia. This can be explained in terms of electron releasing inductive effect of alkyl group. As a result, the electron density on the nitrogen atom increases and thus, they can donate the lone pair of electrons more easily than ammonia.
The electron releasing effect is maximum in tertiary amines and minimum in primary amines (in gas phase).
$3^{\circ}$ amine $>2^{\circ}$ amine $>1^{\circ}$ amine $>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
So, the correct option is (2).

subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question:
Assertion: Basicity of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (I), $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (II) and $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (III) is in the order I $>$ II $>$ III.

Reason: Electron-donating groups (such as alkyl group) increase the basicity of amines, and electron-withdrawing groups (such as aryl group) decrease the basicity of amines.
explanation:
Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.Presence of electron releasing group at benzene nucleus increases the basic character whereas electron withdrawing group decrease the basic character. Hence, correct order of basicity is
${\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_3}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_2} > {\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3} > {{\rm{C}}_6}{{\rm{H}}_5}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_2}$
${\rm{(I)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(II)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(III)}}$

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: Which of the following statements are correct?
explanation: Only statement (3) is correct, whereas statement ( 1 , 2 and 4 ) are incorrect. Basic character depends upon the availability of lone pair of electrons for donation. Aliphatic amines are generally more basic than aromatic amines because lone pair takes part in delocalisation in case of aromatic amines. Presence of electron releasing group at benzene nucleus increases the basic character of amine. Amino group strongly activates the ring through delocalisation of lone pair of electrons of $\mathrm{N}$-atom over the aromatic ring. Thus, electron density increases more at $o$ - and $p$ - positions as compared to $m$-position.

options.text: Aniline is a stronger base than ethyl amine
Aniline is a stronger base than p-methoxyaniline
Aniline must be acetylated before nitration with an acid derivative
Aniline is soluble in an ammonium hydroxide solution

In case of aromatic amines, the lone pair on nitrogen atom are involved in resonance, therefore not available for bond formation, so aromatic amines are less basic.Hence, $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$ is the strongest base among the given options.

question:
Read Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option given below.

Assertion: p-fluoroanilinium ion is more acidic than anilinium ion.

Reason: Electron density in the N-H bond of p-fluoroanilinium ion decreases and release of a proton from p-fluoroanilinium ion is much easier than from anilinium ion.
explanation:
Due to – I effect of F – atom it withdraws electrons from ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}^{+} \mathrm{H}_{3}}}$ group. As a result, electron density in the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}}$ bond of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{p}-}}$ fluoroanilinium ion decreases and hence release of a proton from p - fluoroanilinium ion is much more easier than from anilinium ion.
So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but reason is true.

Aniline does not undergo Friedel Crafts alkylation reaction.
Aniline cannot be prepared through Gabriel synthesis.

explanation:
Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction due to salt formation with aluminium chloride, the Lewis acid, which is used as the catalyst.
Aniline (aromatic primary amine) cannot be prepared by Gabriel phthalimide synthesis because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with anion formed by phthalimide.

question: The correct order in aqueous medium of basic strength in case of methyl substituted amines is
explanation:
In aqueous medium basic strength is dependent on electron density on nitrogen as well as solvation of cation formed after accepting $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ion. Considering the combined effects of solvation and inductive effect, the order of basic strength of methyl amines in the aqueous state is
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}>\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
So, the correct option is (3).

Assertion: Controlled nitration of aniline at low temperature mainly gives m-nitroaniline.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ reacts with $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ whereas $\mathrm{Ph}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ does not.

Reason: The electron pair on nitrogen atom in ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}$ is delocalised in the benzene ring and is not available to boron in $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$.
explanation: Trimethylamine, with three methyl groups, is a strong Lewis base, readily forming a complex with $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$. In contrast, triphenylamine, containing phenyl groups, is less basic due to resonance and less reactive towards Lewis acids like $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ Hence, option (1) is correct.

Reason: Nitrogen is less electronegative than oxygen.
explanation: The higher basicity of ammonia compared to water stems from the lower electronegativity of nitrogen, promoting a greater tendency to donate electrons and form ammonium ions. Thus, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Aniline is a weaker base than cyclohexyl amine.


Reason: Aniline undergoes halogenation even in the absence of a catalyst.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955088/preptracker/questions/q_29022_explanation_1764955087644.png Aniline's resonance hybridization diminishes the availability of nitrogen's lone pair for protonation, stemming from conjugation with the aromatic benzene ring.
Unlike aniline, cyclohexylamine lacks resonance, rendering its nitrogen lone pair more accessible for protonation, making it a stronger base. Given reason is also a correct statement. So the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Aniline is a weaker base than ethylamine.


Reason: Lower the value of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ stronger is the base.
explanation: The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen $(\mathrm{N})$ atom in aniline is delocalized over the benzene ring, and this delocalization reduces its availability for protonation. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955080/preptracker/questions/q_29004_explanation_1764955079888.png  This makes aniline a weaker base than ethylamine, which does not have such extensive electron delocalization. Reason is also true. So the option (2) is correct.

question: An optically active amine $\left(\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{13} \mathrm{~N}\right)$ on treatment with aq. $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$ forms an optically inactive alcohol $\left(\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}\right)$ with evolution of $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas. The amine is
explanation:
Since the amine $\left(\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{13} \mathrm{~N}\right)$ on treatment with aq. $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$ evolves $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas, it must be a $1^{\circ}$ amine. Since the amine is optically active, the $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group cannot be attached to a chiral centre because it will rapidly undergo racemization due to nitrogen inversion.
Therefore, the carbon skeleton must contain a chiral centre, in other words, the amine is 2- Methylbutanamine https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955020/preptracker/questions/q_28866_explanation_1764955020079.png

question: Which among the following reagents is used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary amines according to solubility in alkaline medium?
explanation:
Benzenesulphonyl chloride $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$, (Hinsberg reagent) is used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary amines.
The reaction of benzenesulphonyl chloride with primary amine yields $\mathrm{N}$-alkylbenzene sulphonamide which is soluble in alkali.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{RNH}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{NHR}$
(Soluble in alkali due to presence of acidic hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom.
With secondary amine, it yields N, N dialkylbenzene sulphonamide which is insoluble in alkali.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{NR}_{2}$
(Insoluble in alkali due to absence hydrogen atom attached to nitrogen atom.
Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride.
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl},+\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N} \rightarrow$ No reaction.

question: Correct order of basic strength is
explanation:
Basic nature depends on the ability to donate lone pair of electrons by the atoms present in the species.
Structure (III) is most basic as in that, $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is not directly bonded with the benzene ring. (aliphatic amine)
Structure (II) is 2nd most basic due to presence of $-\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$ group in benzene ring at para position.
Structure (I) is next basic species which is less basic than (III) and (II) but more basic than structure (I) due to presence of electron releasing $\left(-\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)$ group attached to $-\mathrm{NH}$ group. Structure IV is least basic, because
$-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is directly bonded to the benzene ring, thus lone pair of electrons on $\mathrm{N}$ - atom is involved in resonance. Hence correct order is,
(III) $>$ (II) $>$ (I) $>$ (IV) .

Anilinium ion does not show resonance because charge dispersion at ring may involve pentavalent nitrogen structure.

question: The amine 'A' when treated with nitrous acid gives yellow oily substance. The amine $\mathrm{A}$ is
explanation:
Secondary amines when treated with nitrous acid give yellow oily substance. So, amine ' $\mathrm{A}$ ' is methylphenyl amine.
Reaction of nitrous acid with aliphatic amines liberate $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas along with alcohol.
$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{HNO}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \equiv \mathrm{NCl}^{-}$
$\stackrel{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{ROH}+\mathrm{N}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}$
Reaction of nitrous acid with aromatic amines liberates $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ gas along with phenol.
$\mathrm{PhNH}_{2}+\mathrm{HNIO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl} \stackrel{273-276 \mathrm{~K}}{\longrightarrow}$
$\mathrm{Ph}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}} \equiv \mathrm{NCl}^{-1} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PhOH}+\mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$
Reaction of nitrous acid with $2^{\circ}$ amines with
$\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$, they give nitroso amines, which being insoluble in dilute acids separte as yellow oily compound.
$\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}+\mathrm{HONO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{N}$-nitroso dialkyl amine (yellow Oil)
Reaction of nitrous acid with $3^{\circ}$ amines
Aliphatic $3^{\circ}$ amines on reaction with $\mathrm{HONO}$ form soluble nitrite salts, while aromatic $3^{\circ}$ amines form green coloured P-nitroso amines.
${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{\,N + HONO}} \to \mathop {\left[ {{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{3}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{H}}} \right]{\rm{NO}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}}\limits_{\left( {{\rm{Soluble}}\,\,{\rm{nitrite}}} \right)} $
${\rm{Ph - NM}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + HONO}} \to $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949243/preptracker/questions/q_28604_explanation_1764949242932.jpg

question: Which of the following amines is most basic in nature in aqueous phase?
explanation:
Dimethylamine is most basic in nature in aqueous phase. It is more basic as, it is a secondary amine. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is further away from the nucleus at a higher energy level due to extra repulsion from the electrons in each $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{N}$ bond, thus the nitrogen atom is more capable at donating a pair of electrons. So, dimethylamine is a strong base in solution (aqueous phase), as more $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions are formed, when nitrogen atom accepts a proton causing the solution to be more alkaline as the $\mathrm{pH}$ higher.
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NHCH}_{3}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$

+I-effect of alkyl groups on aliphatic amines increase the electron density on nitrogen atom. Aromatic amines are weaker bases due to electron withdrawing nature of the aryl group.
explanation: Aliphatic amines are stronger base than ammonia due to + I-effect of alkyl groups leading to high electron density on the nitrogen atom. Their $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ values lie in the range of 3 to 4.22 which is lower than $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ value of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, i.e. 4.75. On the other hand, aromatic amines are weaker base than ammonia due to the electron withdrawing nature of the aryl group.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

question: A compound with molecular mass $180 u$ is acylated with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl}$ to get a compound with molecular mass $390 u$. The number of amino groups present per molecule of the former compound is
explanation:
During acetylation, one $H$ atom with atomic mass 1 amu of $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group is replaced by an acetyl group $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$ [molecular mass $=434 u]$. Thus, $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCl} \rightarrow-\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}$
The above equation suggests that the acylation of each $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group increases the mass by $42 u[43-1]$. If the molecular mass of the organic compound is $180 u$, while that of the acylated product is $390 u$, then the increase in the mass due to acylation is given by
$390-180=210 u$
Hence, the number of $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ groups $=\dfrac{210 u}{42 u}=5$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Alkylation, Acylation and Benzoylation Reactions

question: Considering the basic strength of amines in aqueous solution, which one has the smallest $p K_{b}$ value?
explanation:
It is known that, $p K_{b}=-\log K_{b}$
Thus, larger the value of $K_{b}$ smaller is the value of $p K_{b}$, hence, stronger is the base.
The order of basic strength of amines is as follows:
${\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{H > C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ > }}{\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{\,}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{ > }}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{6}}}{{\rm{\ddot H}}_{\rm{5}}}{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$
The basic strength of amines depends on the inductive effect as well as steric hindrance of alkyl groups. Hence, ${\left( {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)_{\rm{2}}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{..} {\rm{H}}$ [secondary amine] possesses the smallest value of ${\rm{p}}{{\rm{K}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Basic Character and Effect of Substituents on Basic Nature

explanation: Aromatic amines can indeed be converted into diazonium salts by treatment with nitrous acid, and these salts can then be used to introduce various substituents, including fluorine, into the aromatic ring by reactions such as the Sandmeyer or Schiemann reactions. Correct option is (1)

explanation: In azo coupling reactions, the diazonium group tends to react with the para position due to steric and electronic factors. If the para position is blocked, the reaction can occur at the ortho position. Correct option is (1)

question: The order of stability of below resonance structures is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958649/preptracker/questions/q_107996_explanation_1764958649406.png
Non - charged molecular species are most stable. Therefore, (I) structure gives maximum stability. In II, III resonating structures, stability depends on distance between positive and negative charge. Minimum distance between both charge give maximum coulombic attraction. Thus, (II) resonating structure is more stable than (III). Hence, order of stability will be I $>$ II $>$ III.

question:
Assertion: The reaction between a diazo salt and an aromatic amine or a phenol, giving an aminoazo or hydroxyazo compounds is called coupling reaction.


Reason: Condensation of diazonium salt with phenol is carried out in weakly acidic medium.
explanation: Two aromatic rings joined through the $\mathrm{-N}=\mathrm{N}-$ bond is called as coupling reaction. Phenol molecule at its para position is coupled with the diazonium salt to form $\mathrm{p}$ -hydroxyazobenzene. It takes place in basic medium. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955088/preptracker/questions/q_29038_explanation_1764955088476.png  Hence, option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Dipole moment of p- chloronitrobenzene is less than p-nitrotoluene.


Reason: In p-chloronitrobenzene, both vectors of dipole moment act in opposite direction.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955081/preptracker/questions/q_29008_explanation_1764955080806.png  In p-chloronitrobenzene, the individual dipole moments due to the chlorine atom and nitro group are in opposite directions and hence partially get cancelled. This reduces the overall dipole moment. Whereas, in p-nitrotoluene both the bond dipoles due to the methyl group and the nitro group are in the same direction and get added. So the option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

explanation:
(A) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952323/preptracker/questions/q_108128_explanation_1764952322745.png
(C) Benzenesulphonyl Chloride $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}\right)$ which is also known as Hinsberg's reagent.
(B) $\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group in aniline donates more electrons to the ring than $-\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}$ group so aniline is more reactive than $-\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}$.
Hence, statement (B) is correct.

question: Match the types of reactions involving amines in Column I with the correct product or characteristic in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation: The Carbylamine reaction involves primary amines and produces foul-smelling isocyanides  $({\text{A}} - {\text{S}})$.   The Acylation reaction involves primary or secondary amines and leads to the formation of amides ($\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{R}$).   Electrophilic substitution, such as the bromination of aniline, results in a product like  2, 4, 6 - Tribromoaniline ($\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{P}$). Diazonium salts react with $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$ to replace the diazonium group with hydrogen, forming benzene ($\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{Q}$).   Correct option is (4)

question:
Assertion: Dipole moment of p- chloronitrobenzene is less than p-nitrotoluene.


Reason: In p-chloronitrobenzene, both vectors of dipole moment act in opposite direction.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955081/preptracker/questions/q_29008_explanation_1764955080806.png  In p-chloronitrobenzene, the individual dipole moments due to the chlorine atom and nitro group are in opposite directions and hence partially get cancelled. This reduces the overall dipole moment. Whereas, in p-nitrotoluene both the bond dipoles due to the methyl group and the nitro group are in the same direction and get added. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Conversion of alkyl halide into a cyanide or isocyanide is a nucleophilic substitution reaction.


Reason: When an alkyl halide is treated with alcoholic solution of $\mathrm{AgCN}$, alkyl isocyanides are formed, because in $\mathrm{AgCN}$ only nitrogen is free to donate electron pair.
explanation:
Conversion of alkyl halide into a cyanide or isocyanide is a nucleophilic substitution reaction as it involves the attack on the partially positive carbon atom of the R-X by the - $\mathrm{CN}$ nucleophile. When an alkyl halide is treated with alcoholic solution of $\mathrm{AgCN}$, the product is mainly isonitrile due to the attack of the more nucleophilic nitrogen atom.
$\mathrm{RX}+\mathrm{AgCN} \rightarrow \mathrm{RNC}+\mathrm{AgX}$.
So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Aliphatic hydrocarbons undergo nitration easily with conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ in vapour phase not with nitrating mixture.


Reason: As alkanes do not have delocalized $\pi$-electron cloud to attract positively charged nitronium ion.
explanation:
Nitration of alkanes is achieved through vapor-phase treatment with concentrated nitric acid at elevated temperatures due to the non - polar nature of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ and the weakly polar $\mathrm{C}$-H bonds, preventing attack by polar species under normal conditions.
Therefore, aliphatic hydrocarbons undergo nitration easily with conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ in vapour phase. For example, nitration of methane is as follows:
$\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\underset{\text { Fuming }}{\mathrm{HNO}_{3}} \xrightarrow{670^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
So the option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $1^{\circ}$ or $2^{\circ}$ nitroalkanes dissolve in strong alkalies like $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to form salts.


Reason: Nitroalkanes are weakly acidic in nature.
explanation: Primary and secondary nitroalkanes exhibit weak acidity, allowing them to dissolve in strong alkalies like $\mathrm{NaOH}$, forming salts through removal of $\alpha$-hydrogen by hydroxide ion and resonance stabilization of the resulting carbanion. So the option (1) is correct.

explanation: Nitrobenzene is unreactive in Friedel-Crafts reactions due to the strong deactivating effect of the nitro group, which withdraws electrons, making the benzene ring less attractive to electrophiles. Given reason is true but not the actual explanation. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Tautomerism is exhibited by a compound which is a mixture of two labile forms in dynamic equilibrium.


Reason: Nitroalkanes do not show tautomerism.
explanation: Nitroalkanes containing $\alpha$-hydrogens i.e., $1^{\circ}$ and $2^{\circ}$ (but not $3^{\circ}$ nitroalkanes), show tautomerism. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955078/preptracker/questions/q_28998_explanation_1764955077647.png  Nitroalkanes with $\alpha$-hydrogens, especially primary $\left(1^{\circ}\right)$ and secondary $\left(2^{\circ}\right)$ nitroalkanes, exhibit tautomeric behavior by rapidly interconverting through proton shifts. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Nitrobenzene undergoes electrophilic substitution at meta - position while nucleophilic substitution occurs at ortho - and para - positions.


Reason: Nitro group in benzene ring significantly lowers the electron density at orthoand para - positions, while at meta - position, the electron density is only originally lowered.
explanation: The electron withdrawing effect of the nitro group directs electrophilic substitution reactions to the meta position, where electron density is relatively higher, due to the withdrawal of electrons from the ortho and para positions. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Nitration of benzene and hexadeuterobenzene occur exactly at the same rate.


Reason: The cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is not the rate determining step of the reaction.
explanation: The identical rate of nitration in benzene and hexadeuterobenzene is a result of the primary kinetic isotope effect, where the substitution of hydrogen with deuterium does not significantly impact the reaction rate as cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is not the rate determining step. So option (1) is correct.

explanation:
Due to strong deactivation of benzene ring by $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group, nitrobenzene is often used as a solvent in Friedel - Craft's reaction.
If the reason is taken into consideration then, it is also true because - $\mathrm{OH}$ group is an nucleophilic group and nitrobenzene can undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction and yield a mixture of o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol. So this statement is also true, but assertion and reason are two independent correct statements. So, the correct answer is "option B".

question:
Assertion: When acetamide reacts with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ methyl amine is formed.


Reason: The reaction occurs through intermediate formation of isocyanate.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955102/preptracker/questions/q_29076_explanation_1764955102478.png overall reaction is
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NaBr}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
The Hoffmann bromamide degradation of acetamide involves the formation of methyl isocyanate as an intermediate, which, upon hydrolysis, yields methylamine and sodium carbonate.. Thus, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question:
Assertion: Acetamide reacts with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in presence of methanolic $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{ONa}$ to form methyl $\mathrm{N}$-methylcarbamate.


Reason: Methyl isocyanate is formed as an intermediate which reacts with methanol to form methyl N-methylcarbamate.
explanation: Hofmann bromamide reaction is a reaction in which amides are converted to amines with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in the presence of a base. The reaction proceeds through the formation of isocyanate as an intermediate. It then forms amine upon hydrolysis. Here, the base is methanolic $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{ONa}$. Therefore, option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question:
Assertion: In Hoffmann bromamide reaction. the amine formed has one carbon atom less than the parent $1^{\circ}$ amide

Reason: N-methyl acetamide undergoes
Hoffmann bromamide reaction.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955079/preptracker/questions/q_29000_explanation_1764955078520.png It is a method for preparation of primary amines only. In this degradation reaction, migration of an alkyl or aryl group takes place from carbonyl carbon of the amide to the nitrogen atom. The amine so formed contains one carbon less than that present in the amide. Only primary amides will give this reaction.
$\mathrm{N}$-methyl acetamide $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONHCH}_{3}\right)$ is a secondary amide and cannot give Hoffmann bromamide reaction, as it cannot form the reaction intermediate. So the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955077/preptracker/questions/q_28986_explanation_1764955076830.png  Ammonolysis of alkyl or benzyl halides in ethanolic ammonia results in nucleophilic substitution, replacing the halogen with an amino group, leading to the formation of primary amines that can further react to produce secondary, tertiary amines, and quaternary ammonium salt. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Alkyl cyanides on reduction with $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Ni}$ give $1^{\circ}$ amine.

Reason: Cyanides and isocyanides are functional isomers of each other.
explanation:
$\mathrm{RC} \equiv \mathrm{N} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{Hg} / \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}]{\stackrel{\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{i}}{\longrightarrow}} \mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$
Nitriles on reduction with lithium aluminium hydride $\left(\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}\right)$ or catalytic hydrogenation produce primary amines. Cyanides $(\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CN})$ and isocyanides (R-NC) are indeed functional isomers. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Identify the compounds from the following which form primary amines under suitable reduction conditions:
(I) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NC}$ (II) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}$
(III) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}$ (IV) $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$
explanation:
Hofmann bromamide degradation reaction gives primary amines.
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+ & 4 \mathrm{KOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{NH}_{2} \\& +2 \mathrm{KBr}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
Primary amines may also be prepared by the reduction of nitroalkanes, nitrobenzene etc.
${\text{RN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow[\substack{ {\text{ or}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{LAH}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{or}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{/Pt }} \\ {\text{ or Metal}}{\mkern 1mu} {\text{acid }} } ]{{{\text{Ni/250}}^\circ {\text{C}}}}{\text{RN}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ + 2}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
${{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_2} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ LAH }}}]{{{\text{Ni}}/250^\circ {\text{C}}}}{{\text{C}}_6}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$

explanation:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I} \rightarrow\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$
$\stackrel{+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}}{\longrightarrow}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N} \stackrel{+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{I}}{\longrightarrow}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+} \mathrm{I}^{-}$

question: An alkyl halide reacts with alcoholic ammonia in a sealed tube, the product formed will be a
explanation:
Alkyl halide racts with alcoholic ammonia gives the mixture of amines.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{RX}+\mathrm{NH}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{RNH}_{2}+\mathrm{HX} \\& \mathrm{RNH}_{2}+\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}+\mathrm{HX} \\& \mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}+\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N}+\mathrm{HX} \\& \mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N}+\mathrm{X}-\mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{4} \mathrm{~N}^{+} \mathrm{X}^{-}\end{aligned}$

options.text: a mixture of all the three

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation from Nitro, Alkyl Halides, Nitriles, Amides

question: Alkyl cyanides on reduction by sodium and ethanol give primary amines. This reaction is called as
explanation:
${\text{R - }}\mathop {{\text{C}} \equiv {\text{N + 4}}}\limits_{{\text{Alkyl cyanide}}} \left[ {\text{H}} \right]\xrightarrow{{{\text{Na Ethanol}}}}{\text{R - }}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{ - N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}}\limits_{{\text{Primary amine}}} $
Given, reaction is called Mendius reduction. In this reaction the reduction of cyano group $(-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N})$ takes place by nascent hydrogen in the presence of sodium and alcohol. Here nascent hydrogen breaks $\mathrm{X}$ bond of cyano group.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Named Preparation Methods of Amines

question: Which of the following statement(s) is/are incorrect in case of Hofmann bromamide degradation?
explanation:
Statement (B) is incorrect in case of Hofmann bromanide degradation. This is used to synthesise primary amine.
In this reaction, amide on reaction with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ in an aqueous or ethanolic solution of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ give $1^{\circ}$ amine with one $\mathrm{C}$ atom less than that present in the amine.
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{RCONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{KOH} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \underset{1^{\circ} \mathrm{a} \text { mine }}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}+2 \mathrm{KBr}} \\+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{gathered}$

options.text: Reaction is useful for decreasing length of carbon chain by one carbon atom

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question: Hoffmann's bromamide reaction is to convert
explanation: Hoffmann's bromamide reaction: Amides when heated with bromine and caustic soda or caustic potash solution, yield primary amine containing one carbon atom less than the amide.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question: Which of the following would undergo Hoffmann reaction to give a primary amine?
explanation:
When a primary amide is heated with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{KOH}$ and $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$, it gives a primary amine with one carbon atom less.
$\begin{aligned}& \underset{\text { amide }}{\mathrm{RCONH}_{2}}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{KOH} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \\& \underset{1^{\circ} \text { amine }}{\mathrm{RNH}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{KBr}+\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
This is known as Hoffmann degradation reaction.

subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

question:
$\mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{HCl}$ is preferred as a reagent in the oxidation of nitroalkanes to alkanamines.
Iron scrap and $\mathrm{HCl}$ is preferred in the reduction reaction of nitrobenzene.
explanation:
Reduction of nitro compounds with Fe scrap and $\mathrm{HCl}$ is preferred because $\mathrm{FeCl}_{2}$ formed gets hydrolysed to release $\mathrm{HCl}$ during the reaction.
Thus, only a small amount of $\mathrm{HCl}$ is required to initiate the reaction.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: An organic compound $\mathrm{A}$ on reacting with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives $\mathrm{B}$. On heating $\mathrm{B}$, it gives $\mathrm{C}$. $\mathrm{C}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{KOH}$ reacts with $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ to give $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$. A is
explanation:
$\mathrm{A} \stackrel{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{B} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{C} \stackrel{\mathrm{KOH} / \mathrm{Br}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{NH}_{2}$
The reaction of $\mathrm{C}$ with $\mathrm{KOH} / \mathrm{Br}_{2}$ to give amine is called Hoffmann bromamide reaction. this reaction is given by acid amides only in which https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/15/ES-Q-44_(1).jpg group undergoes rearrangement along with the loss of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ molecule. Thus, the
compound $\mathrm{C}$ must be acid amide with three carbon atoms. Hence, the compound $\mathrm{C}$ is https://image.cleverb.in/upload/2023/09/15/ES-Q-44_(2).jpg Since, all the options show that $\mathrm{A}$ is an acid and it forms acid amide on reaction with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$. Thus, acid must contain three carbon atoms. Hence the compound $\mathrm{A}$ is $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$. The complete series of reactions can be represented as
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{COOH}}}\limits_{\left( {\text{A}} \right)} \xrightarrow{{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}}\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\text{ + }} {{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}}\limits_{\left( {\text{B}} \right)} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949112/preptracker/questions/q_28253_explanation_1764949111894.jpg
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis and Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation Reaction

explanation: Aniline cannot be prepared by this method because aryl halides do not undergo nucleophilic substitution with potassium phthalimide under ordinary conditions to give $\mathrm{N}$ phenyl phthalimide (i.e., cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond in haloarenes is quite difficult).

question:
An alkyl amine is prepared by the following reaction
$\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{N}_{3} \mathrm{H} \stackrel{\text { Conc. } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{RNH}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{N}_{2}$

The above reaction is known as Schmidt reaction. This is an important method of preparing primary amines.
